question: Explain the primary difference between a stack and a queue. Provide a real-world example for each data structure.
expectedPoints: Stack is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) structure, meaning the last element added is the first one removed.
Queue is a FIFO (First-In, First-Out) structure, meaning the first element added is the first one removed.
Real-world example for stack: the 'undo' function in an application, where the last action is the first one undone.
Real-world example for queue: a print job queue, where documents are printed in the order they were sent.

question: Describe the process of inserting a new node into a Binary Search Tree (BST). Explain how the tree maintains its ordering property and provide a brief example.
expectedPoints: Start at the root node and compare the new value with the current node's value.
If the new value is less, move to the left child; if greater, move to the right child.
Repeat this process until an empty spot (null pointer) is found, then insert the new node there.
This process maintains the BST property where all left descendants are less than the node, and all right descendants are greater.
Example: Inserting the value 3 into a BST with root 5 would place it as the left child of 5.

question: Compare and contrast the Adjacency Matrix and Adjacency List representations for graphs. Discuss the time and space complexity trade-offs for common operations like finding all adjacent nodes or checking for an edge between two nodes.
expectedPoints: An adjacency matrix uses a VxV 2D array, where a value at matrix[i][j] indicates an edge between vertex i and j.
An adjacency list uses an array of lists (e.g., linked lists), where each list stores the neighbors of a vertex.
Space Complexity: Matrix is O(V²); List is O(V + E), making the list more efficient for sparse graphs.
Check for an edge: Matrix is O(1) constant time; List is O(degree(V)) in the worst case.
Find all adjacent nodes: Matrix is O(V) as it must scan a whole row; List is O(degree(V)) as it simply traverses the list.
Conclusion: Matrix is better for dense graphs and edge lookup, while List is better for sparse graphs and memory efficiency.
